name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَيَسْتَفْتُونَكَ فِى ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِيهِنَّ وَمَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ فِى يَتَـٰمَى ٱلنِّسَآءِ ٱلَّـٰتِى لَا تُؤْتُونَهُنَّ مَا كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ وَتَرْغَبُونَ أَن تَنكِحُوهُنَّ وَٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ ٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ وَأَن تَقُومُوا۟ لِلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمًا (١٢٧)
```

```english
They seek your ruling concerning women. Say, ‘Allah gives you a ruling concerning them and what is announced to you in the Book concerning girl orphans—whom you do not give what has been prescribed for them, and yet you desire to marry them—and about the weak among children: that you should maintain the orphans with justice, and whatever good you do, indeed Allah knows it well. (127)
```

```arabic
وَإِنِ ٱمْرَأَةٌ خَافَتْ مِنۢ بَعْلِهَا نُشُوزًا أَوْ إِعْرَاضًا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَآ أَن يُصْلِحَا بَيْنَهُمَا صُلْحًا ۚ وَٱلصُّلْحُ خَيْرٌ ۗ وَأُحْضِرَتِ ٱلْأَنفُسُ ٱلشُّحَّ ۚ وَإِن تُحْسِنُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (١٢٨)
```

```english
If a woman fears from her husband misconduct or desertion, there is no sin upon the couple if they reach a reconciliation between themselves; and reconcilement is better. The souls are prone to greed; but if you are virtuous and Godwary, Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (128)
```

```arabic
وَلَن تَسْتَطِيعُوٓا۟ أَن تَعْدِلُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ ۖ فَلَا تَمِيلُوا۟ كُلَّ ٱلْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوهَا كَٱلْمُعَلَّقَةِ ۚ وَإِن تُصْلِحُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٢٩)
```

```english
You will not be able to be fair between wives, even if you are eager to do so. Yet do not turn away from one altogether, leaving her as if in a suspense. But if you are conciliatory and Godwary, Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (129)
```

```arabic
وَإِن يَتَفَرَّقَا يُغْنِ ٱللَّهُ كُلًّا مِّن سَعَتِهِۦ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعًا حَكِيمًا (١٣٠)
```

```english
But if they separate, Allah will suffice each of them out of His bounty, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-wise. (130)
```

```arabic
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَلَقَدْ وَصَّيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ أَنِ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ وَإِن تَكْفُرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَنِيًّا حَمِيدًا (١٣١)
```

```english
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. We have certainly enjoined those who were given the Book before you, and you, that you should be wary of Allah. But if you are faithless, [you should know that] to Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable. (131)
```

```arabic
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (١٣٢)
```

```english
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah suffices as trustee. (132)
```

```arabic
إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَيَأْتِ بِـَٔاخَرِينَ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ قَدِيرًا (١٣٣)
```

```english
If He wishes, He will take you away, O mankind, and bring others [in your place]; Allah has the power to do that. (133)
```

```arabic
مَّن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ثَوَابَ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَعِندَ ٱللَّهِ ثَوَابُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا (١٣٤)
```

```english
Whoever desires the reward of this world, [should know that] with Allah is the reward of this world and the Hereafter, and Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (134)
```

## (Commentary)

The discourse is linked to the verses at the beginning of the Surah concerning women—verses regarding marriage, prohibitions, inheritance, and other matters. The context indicates that these verses were revealed after those initial ones, and that the people questioned the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the affairs of women when the verses at the start of the Surah were revealed. Those earlier verses had revived the rights of women in property, social interactions, and other areas that the people had previously suppressed.

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, commanded him to answer them that the rulings He had established for women against men are but a divine decree (فتيا إلهية); he has no personal authority in the matter. Furthermore, it is not only that, but what is recited to them in the Book concerning orphan women is also a divine ruling in which the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has no personal authority. Beyond that, Allah commands them to maintain justice (القسط) toward orphans.

Then, He mentioned some rulings regarding disputes between a woman and her husband, as this is a widespread trial (يعم به البلوى).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَسْتَفْتُونَكَ فِي النِّساءِ قُلِ اللهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِيهِنَّ** — "They seek your ruling concerning women. Say, ‘Allah gives you a ruling concerning them...’" (4:127). Al-Raghib said: "The formal legal opinion (الفتيا) and the ruling (الفتوى) are the answer to problematic legal issues. It is said: 'I sought a ruling from him (استفتيته) and he gave me a ruling (أفتاني) such and such.'" (End of quote).

What is gathered from its usage is that it is a person's answer to problematic matters based on what he perceives through the exertion of his own judgment (اجتهاد), or it is the very view he holds regarding a problematic issue according to an initial, simple perspective—as suggested by attributing the ruling (الفتوى) to Him, the Exalted.

Although the verse allows for various meanings depending on the different grammatical views mentioned regarding the structure of the subsequent phrase: **وَما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ فِي يَتامَى النِّساءِ** — "...and what is recited to you in the Book concerning orphan women..." (4:127), the connection of this verse to the verses concerning women at the beginning of the Surah testifies that this verse was revealed after those.

The implication of this is that their seeking of a ruling (استفتاؤهم) regarding women concerned the general innovations and unprecedented rulings Islam introduced regarding them—things that were not customary or known to them in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). This pertains specifically to the rights of women in inheritance and marriage, rather than the rulings of their orphans and other matters that concern only a specific group of them. This latter meaning is addressed by His saying: **وَما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ فِي يَتامَى النِّساءِ** — "...and what is recited to you in the Book concerning orphan women..." (4:127). Thus, the seeking of a ruling (الاستفتاء) was about what pertains to women as women regarding the laws of inheritance.

Accordingly, what is meant by that which Allah gave a ruling on in His saying: **قُلِ اللهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِيهِنَّ** — "Say, ‘Allah gives you a ruling concerning them...’" (4:127) is what He, the Exalted, explained in the verses at the beginning of the Surah. The discourse then serves to refer the matter of the ruling back to Allah, the Glorified, and away from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The meaning is: They ask you to give them a ruling regarding their affair; say: The ruling belongs to Allah, and He has already given you a ruling concerning them in what He revealed of the verses at the beginning of the Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ فِي يَتامَى النِّساءِ** — "...and what is recited to you in the Book concerning orphan women..." until His saying: **وَالْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الْوِلْدانِ** — "...and the oppressed children" (4:127). It was previously mentioned that the apparent context suggests that the ruling on orphan women and oppressed children was addressed because of its connection to the ruling on women—as occurred in the verses at the start of the Surah—not because it was part of what they sought a ruling on. They only sought a ruling concerning women.

The implication is that His saying: **وَما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ** — "...and what is recited to you..." is conjoined to the genitive pronoun in His saying: **فِيهِنَّ** — "...concerning them," as permitted by al-Farra’, even if the majority of grammarians forbid it. Based on this, the meaning of His saying: **ما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ فِي يَتامَى النِّساءِ** — "...what is recited to you in the Book concerning orphan women..." refers to the rulings and meanings contained in the verses revealed regarding orphan women and children placed at the beginning of the Surah. Recitation (التلاوة), just as it is applied to the wording, is also applied to the meaning when it underlies the wording. The meaning is: Say, Allah gives you a ruling regarding the laws that are recited to you in the Book concerning orphan women.

It may appear from some that they conjoin His saying: **وَما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ** — "...and what is recited to you..." to the position of His saying: **فِيهِنَّ** — "...concerning them," with the consideration that the meaning of giving a ruling (الإفتاء) is clarification (التبيين). The meaning would be: Say, Allah clarifies for you what is recited to you in the Book.

Other grammatical structures have been mentioned for the discourse, but they are not free from a degree of forced interpretation (تعسف) that should not be attributed to His speech, the Exalted. For instance, some say that His saying: **وَما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ** — "...and what is recited to you..." is conjoined to the position of the Majestic Name in His saying: **قُلِ اللهُ** — "Say, ‘Allah...’", or to the latent pronoun in His saying: **يُفْتِيكُمْ** — "...gives you a ruling." Others say it is conjoined to **النِّساءِ** — "women" in His saying: **فِي النِّساءِ** — "concerning women." Some say the "and" (الواو) in His saying: **وَما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ** — "...and what is recited to you in the Book..." is for commencement (للاستيناف), making the sentence independent, where **ما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ** — "...what is recited to you..." is the subject (مبتدأ) and its predicate (خبر) is His saying: **فِي الْكِتابِ** — "...in the Book," and the discourse is intended for glorification. Others say the "and" (الواو) is for an oath (للقسم), and His saying: **فِي يَتامَى النِّساءِ** — "...concerning orphan women..." is a substitute (بدل) for His saying: **فِيهِنَّ** — "...concerning them." The meaning would be: Say, Allah gives you a ruling—I swear by what is recited to you in the Book—concerning orphan women... It is not hidden that all these views contain obvious forced interpretation.

As for His saying: **اللَّاتِي لا تُؤْتُونَهُنَّ ما كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ وَتَرْغَبُونَ أَنْ تَنْكِحُوهُنَّ** — "...whom you do not give what has been prescribed for them, and whom you desire to marry..." (4:127), it is a description of the orphan women. It points to the type of deprivation they faced, which was the reason for the legislation Allah, the Exalted, enacted for them, thereby abolishing the unjust custom (السنة الجائرة) practiced against them and removing their hardship. This was because they used to take orphan women and their wealth; if she was beautiful, they would marry her to enjoy her beauty and wealth, and if she was ugly or deformed, they would not marry her and would prevent her from marrying others out of greed for her wealth.

From here it becomes clear, firstly: that what is meant by His saying: **ما كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ** — "...what has been prescribed for them" is the ontological prescription (الكتابة التكوينية)—that is, the divine decree (التقدير الإلهي). For creation and origination (الصنع والإيجاد) are what carve the path of life for a human being, determining that one should marry upon reaching maturity and act freely with one's property and possessions. Preventing her from marriage and from disposing of her own property is a prevention of what Allah has prescribed for her in creating her with this nature.

Secondly: the omitted preposition in His saying: **أَنْ تَنْكِحُوهُنَّ** — "...to marry them" is the word "from" (عن), and the meaning is "desiring *against* (الراغبة عن) marrying them"—that is, turning away from them, not desiring to marry them. Mentioning the desire *against* them is more appropriate to indicate their deprivation, as evidenced by His preceding saying: **لا تُؤْتُونَهُنَّ ما كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ** — "...you do not give what has been prescribed for them," and His subsequent saying: **وَالْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الْوِلْدانِ** — "...and the oppressed children."

As for His saying: **وَالْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الْوِلْدانِ** — "...and the oppressed children," it is conjoined to His saying: **يَتامَى النِّساءِ** — "...orphan women." They used to oppress the children among the orphans and deprive them of inheritance, making the excuse that they do not ride horses nor defend the womenfolk.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنْ تَقُومُوا لِلْيَتامى بِالْقِسْطِ** — "...and that you maintain justice for the orphans" (4:127) is conjoined to the position of His saying: **فِيهِنَّ** — "...concerning them." The meaning is: Say, Allah gives you a ruling that you maintain justice for the orphans. This is like a transition (إضراب) from a specific ruling to a more general one—that is, moving from the ruling of some orphan women and children to the ruling of orphans in general regarding their wealth and other matters.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما تَفْعَلُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِهِ عَلِيماً** — "And whatever good you do, Allah is indeed all-knowing of it" (4:127) is a reminder to them that the rulings Allah has mandated for them regarding women and orphans contain their own good, and that Allah is all-knowing of it. This serves as an encouragement for them to act upon it because their good lies within it, and a warning against opposing it because Allah is all-knowing of what they do.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنِ امْرَأَةٌ خافَتْ مِنْ بَعْلِها نُشُوزاً أَوْ إِعْراضاً** — "If a woman fears recalcitrance or disregard from her husband..." (4:128) is a ruling outside of what they sought a ruling on, but it is connected to it by association, similar to the ruling mentioned in the following verse: **وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا** — "You will not be able..." (4:129).

The "fear" (خوف) of recalcitrance (النشوز) — *the husband's arrogant or hostile behavior toward his wife* — and disregard (الإعراض) — *his turning away from her* — was considered rather than their actual occurrence because the subject of reconciliation (الصلح) is established from the moment the signs and effects leading to fear appear. The context indicates that what is meant by reconciliation is the woman waiving some or all of her marital rights to foster intimacy, affection, and harmony, and to guard against the occurrence of separation; and reconciliation is better.

And His saying: **وَأُحْضِرَتِ الْأَنْفُسُ الشُّحَّ** — "...and souls are prone to greed" (4:128). Greed (الشح) is stinginess (البخل). Its meaning is that greed is one of the psychological instincts (الغرائز النفسانية) that Allah ingrained in souls to preserve their interests and protect them from loss. Thus, the greed inherent in every soul is present within it. The woman is stingy with her marital rights, such as clothing, maintenance, the marriage bed, and sexual intercourse; and the man is stingy with harmony and inclination if he desires separation and dislikes companionship. There is no blame on them then if they reconcile between themselves by one or both of them overlooking some of their rights.

Then He, the Exalted, said: **وَإِنْ تُحْسِنُوا وَتَتَّقُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً** — "But if you be virtuous and godwary, Allah is indeed all-aware of what you do" (4:128). This is an exhortation to men not to transgress the path of virtue (الإحسان) and godwariness (التقوى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — and to remember that Allah is all-aware of what they do. They should not be unjust in companionship, nor should they coerce women into waiving their rightful rights, even if the women have the right to do so.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ** — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager..." (4:129) is an explanation of the ruling of justice (العدل) between wives that was legislated for them against men in His saying, the Exalted, at the beginning of the Surah: **فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً** — "...but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one..." (4:3). Similarly, His saying in the previous verse: **وَإِنْ تُحْسِنُوا وَتَتَّقُوا** — "But if you be virtuous and godwary..." (4:128) points to this, as it is not free from a hint of threat. This necessitates confusion in identifying the reality of justice between them. Justice (العدل) is the middle point between excess (الإفراط) and negligence (التفريط), and it is extremely difficult to identify, especially regarding the heart's attachment in terms of love, for heart-felt love is not always subject to choice.

Thus, He, the Exalted, explained that justice between wives in its true sense—which is maintaining the exact middle point—is something a human being is unable to do, even if he is eager for it. What is mandatory for a man is only that he does not incline completely toward one side, especially the side of negligence, thereby leaving the woman like one suspended (كالمعلقة)—neither having a husband from whom she benefits, nor being a widow so she may marry or go about her business.

Therefore, the mandatory justice for a man between wives is to treat them equally in practice by giving them their rights without deviation. What is recommended (المندوب) for him is to be virtuous toward them, not to show dislike for their companionship, and not to treat them with bad character. Such was the practice (سيرة) of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

This concluding part—that is, His saying: **فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ** — "...so do not incline with a total inclination, leaving her like one suspended" (4:129)—is the proof that His saying: **وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ** — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager..." (4:129) does not mean the negation of absolute justice such that, when joined with His saying: **فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً** — "...but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one..." (4:3), it would result in the abolition of polygyny in Islam, as has been claimed.

This is because the concluding part indicates that what is negated is the true, ontological justice (العدل الحقيقي الواقعي) without any deviation whatsoever by adhering to the exact middle point, and that what is legislated is approximate justice (العدل التقريبي) in practice without undue hardship.

Furthermore, the Prophetic Sunnah (السنة النبوية), the prevalence of the matter in the sight and hearing of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the continuous practice (السيرة المتصلة) among Muslims refute this delusion.

Moreover, to interpret His saying in the first verse of polygyny: **فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ** — "...then marry of women as may be agreeable to you, two, or three, or four..." (4:3) as a mere intellectual hypothesis devoid of any actual application is nothing but an obfuscation from which His speech, the Glorified, is far removed.

Then His saying: **وَإِنْ تُصْلِحُوا وَتَتَّقُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً** — "And if you come to an agreement and be godwary, Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful" (4:129) is a confirmation and encouragement for men to reconcile when signs of dislike and discord appear, by explaining that it is part of godwariness (التقوى), and godwariness entails forgiveness and mercy. This, coming after His saying: **وَالصُّلْحُ خَيْرٌ** — "...and reconciliation is better" (4:128) and His saying: **وَإِنْ تُحْسِنُوا وَتَتَّقُوا** — "But if you be virtuous and godwary..." (4:128), is a confirmation upon confirmation.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ يَتَفَرَّقا يُغْنِ اللهُ كُلًّا مِنْ سَعَتِهِ** — "But if they separate, Allah will provide for each out of His bounty..." (4:130). That is, if the man and woman separate through divorce, Allah will enrich each of them from His vastness. Enrichment (الإغناء), by the context of the situation, refers to enrichment in everything related to marriage—harmony, intimacy, physical contact, and the wife's clothing and maintenance. For Allah did not create one of these two spouses solely for the other such that if they separated, neither would find another spouse for the rest of their lives; rather, this custom is a primordial custom (سنة فطرية) widespread among the individuals of this species, toward which every individual inclines according to their primordial nature (فطرة).

And His saying: **وَكانَ اللهُ واسِعاً حَكِيماً وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "...and Allah is all-bounteous, all-wise. To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (4:130-131) is a justification for the ruling mentioned in His saying: **يُغْنِ اللهُ كُلًّا مِنْ سَعَتِهِ** — "...Allah will provide for each out of His bounty."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ وَصَّيْنَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ أَنِ اتَّقُوا اللهَ** — "We have certainly enjoined those who were given the Book before you, and you, that you should be godwary of Allah..." (4:131) is a confirmation in calling them to observe the quality of godwariness (التقوى) in all stages of marital companionship and in every state. Neglecting it constitutes ingratitude (كفر) for Allah's blessing, based on the fact that godwariness achieved through obedience to Allah is nothing but gratitude for His blessings; or that the neglect of godwariness toward Allah, the Exalted, has no source other than disbelief (الكفر)—either manifest disbelief as in the disbelievers and polytheists, or latent, internal disbelief as in the transgressors among the believers.

By what we have explained, the meaning of His saying: **وَإِنْ تَكْفُرُوا فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "...But if you are ungrateful, then to Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth..." (4:131) becomes clear. That is: if you do not preserve what We have enjoined upon you and those before you, and you waste this testament and are not godwary—which is disbelief in Allah or stems from disbelief in Allah—then that does not harm Allah, the Glorified, as He has no need of you or your godwariness; to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and Allah is all-sufficient (غني), all-laudable (حميد).

If you ask: What is the reason for the repetition of His saying: **لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth"? For it has been mentioned three times.

I say: As for the first, it is a justification for His saying: **وَكانَ اللهُ واسِعاً حَكِيماً** — "...and Allah is all-bounteous, all-wise." As for the second, it stands in the place of the response to the condition (جواب الشرط) in His saying: **وَإِنْ تَكْفُرُوا** — "...But if you are ungrateful," and the implied meaning is: "If you are ungrateful, then He is self-sufficient (غني) without you," and it is a justification for the response, which has appeared in His saying: **وَكانَ اللهُ غَنِيًّا حَمِيداً** — "...and Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable."

As for the third, it is a commencement (استيناف) and a justification in a way for His saying: **إِنْ يَشَأْ** — "If He wishes..."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَكَفى بِاللهِ وَكِيلاً** — "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and Allah suffices as a guardian" (4:132). The meaning of His ownership (ملك) has been explained repeatedly. He, the Exalted, is a Guardian (وكيل) Who manages the affairs and concerns of His servants, and He suffices as a Guardian, needing no support or assistance. Thus, if He were not pleased with the deeds of a people and their conduct angered Him, He could remove them and bring others, or delay them and bring others forward. With this meaning, which is supported and indeed indicated by the context, His saying in the following verse is linked: **إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ** — "If He wishes, He will take you away, O mankind..." (4:133).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ وَيَأْتِ بِآخَرِينَ** — "If He wishes, He will take you away, O mankind, and bring others..." (4:133). The context—which is the call to adhere to godwariness (التقوى) that Allah enjoined upon this nation and those before them of the People of the Book—indicates that the manifestation of self-sufficiency and lack of need signified by His saying: **إِنْ يَشَأْ** — "If He wishes" pertains to the matter of godwariness.

The meaning is that Allah enjoined you all to adhere to godwariness, so be godwary of Him. If you disbelieve, He is self-sufficient without you; He is the Owner of everything, disposing of it however He wishes and for whatever He wishes. If He wishes to be worshipped and feared, and you do not fulfill that as it should be fulfilled, He is capable of delaying you and bringing forward others who will fulfill what He loves and is pleased with; and Allah is all-powerful over that.

Accordingly, the verse looks toward replacing the people—if they are not godwary—with other people who will be godwary of Allah. It has been narrated (1) that when the verse was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) struck his hand on Salman's back and said: "They are the people of this one." This supports this meaning, and you should reflect upon it.

As for what some commentators have suggested—that the meaning is: "If He wishes, He will annihilate you and create another people in your place or another creation in place of humanity"—this meaning is far from the context. Yes, it is acceptable in verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بِالْحَقِّ إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ وَما ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ بِعَزِيزٍ** — "Have you not regarded that Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth? If He wishes, He will take you away, and bring a new creation, and that is not a great matter for Allah" (14:19-20).

His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ ثَوابَ الدُّنْيا فَعِنْدَ اللهِ ثَوابُ الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَكانَ اللهُ سَمِيعاً بَصِيراً** — "Whoever desires the reward of this world, with Allah is the reward of this world and the Hereafter, and Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing" (4:134). This is another explanation clarifying the error of one who abandons godwariness toward Allah and wastes His testament. If he does so seeking the reward and gain of this world, the matter has become confused for him, for the reward of both this world and the Hereafter together is with Allah and in His hand. Why then does he restrict his vision to the baser of the two and not seek the more noble of them, or both of them together? So it has been said.

What is more apparent—and Allah knows best—is that the meaning is that the reward of this world and the Hereafter, and the felicity of both together, is only with Allah, the Glorified. So let one seek nearness to Him, even if he desires the reward and felicity of this world. For felicity is not found for a human being except in the godwariness of Allah achieved through His religion which He legislated for him. Religion is nothing but the path to true felicity. How then can anyone attain a reward without His granting and bestowing it from Himself? And Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Sa'id ibn Jubayr, who said: "None used to inherit except the man who had reached maturity—to manage the wealth and work with it; neither the child nor the woman would inherit anything. When the [verses of] inheritance were revealed in Surah al-Nisa’, that became difficult for the people, and they said: 'Shall the child inherit who does not manage wealth, and the woman who is likewise, inheriting just as the man inherits?' They hoped that some new decree (حدث) would come from heaven regarding that, so they waited. When they saw that no new decree came, they said: 'If this continues, it is an obligation from which there is no escape.' Then they said: 'Ask,' so they asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and Allah revealed: **وَيَسْتفتُونَكَ فِي النِّساءِ قُلِ اللهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِيهِنَّ** — 'They seek your ruling concerning women. Say, "Allah gives you a ruling concerning them..."' — **وَما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ** — '...and what is recited to you in the Book...' — at the beginning of the Surah — **فِي يَتامَى النِّساءِ اللَّاتِي لا تُؤْتُونَهُنَّ ما كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ وَتَرْغَبُونَ أَنْ تَنْكِحُوهُنَّ** — '...concerning orphan women whom you do not give what has been prescribed for them, and whom you desire to marry...'" (The hadith).

In the same source: 'Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibrahim regarding the verse, who said: "If a girl was an orphan and ugly, they would not give her her inheritance and would prevent her from marriage until she died, so they could inherit from her. So Allah revealed this."

I say: These meanings are narrated through many chains from both Shi'i and Sunni sources, and some of them were mentioned at the beginning of the Surah.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لا تُؤْتُونَهُنَّ ما كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ** — "...whom you do not give what has been prescribed for them" (4:127), [meaning] what was prescribed for them of inheritance, he said: "It is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him)."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنِ امْرَأَةٌ خافَتْ مِنْ بَعْلِها نُشُوزاً** — "If a woman fears recalcitrance from her husband..." (4:128): "It was revealed concerning the daughter of Muhammad ibn Maslamah—she was the wife of Rafi' ibn Khadij. She was a woman who had advanced in age, and he married a young woman in addition to her, and she was more pleasing to him than the daughter of Muhammad ibn Maslamah. The daughter of Muhammad ibn Maslamah said to him: 'Do I not see you turning away from me and preferring her over me?' Rafi' said: 'She is a young woman and she is more pleasing to me. If you wish, I will keep you on the condition that she has two or three days from me, and you have one day.' The daughter of Muhammad ibn Maslamah refused to be satisfied, so he divorced her once, then divorced her again. She said: 'No, by Allah, I will not be satisfied unless you treat me and her equally.' Allah says: **وَأُحْضِرَتِ الْأَنْفُسُ الشُّحَّ** — '...and souls are prone to greed.' The daughter of Muhammad did not seek her own portion and was greedy for him. Rafi' offered her either to be satisfied or he would divorce her the third time. She was greedy for her husband and became satisfied, so she reconciled with him on what was mentioned. Allah said: **فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما أَنْ يُصْلِحا بَيْنَهُما صُلْحاً وَالصُّلْحُ خَيْرٌ** — '...there is no blame on them if they reconcile between themselves, and reconciliation is better.' When she was satisfied and settled, he was unable to be just between them, so it was revealed: **وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ** — 'You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager. So do not incline with a total inclination, leaving her like one suspended'—that he goes to one and leaves the other, neither unmarried nor having a husband. This is the Sunnah in such cases: if the woman agrees and is satisfied with what her husband reconciled with her upon, there is no blame on the husband or the woman. If she refuses, he divorces her or treats them equally; he has no other choice."

I say: It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Malik, 'Abd al-Razzaq, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—in a summarized form.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Tayalisi, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Rahwayh, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from 'Ali ibn Abi Talib: that he was asked about this verse and said: "It is a man who has two wives, and one of them has become old or is ugly and he wants to separate from her. She reconciles with him on the condition that he stays with her for one night and with the other for several nights and does not separate from her. Whatever she is pleased with, there is no harm in it. If she changes her mind, he must treat them equally."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَإِنِ امْرَأَةٌ خافَتْ مِنْ بَعْلِها نُشُوزاً أَوْ إِعْراضاً** — 'If a woman fears recalcitrance or disregard from her husband...' (4:128). He said: 'It is the woman who is with a man and he dislikes her, so he says to her: "I want to divorce you." She says to him: "Do not do so, for I hate that people should gloat over me. But look at my night and do with it as you wish, and whatever else there is, it is for you, and leave me as I am." That is the saying of Him, the Blessed and Exalted: **فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما أَنْ يُصْلِحا بَيْنَهُما صُلْحاً** — "...there is no blame on them if they reconcile between themselves." And this is the reconciliation.'"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning in *al-Kafi* and in *Tafsir al-'Ayyashi*.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَأُحْضِرَتِ الْأَنْفُسُ الشُّحَّ** — "...and souls are prone to greed" (4:128), he said: "He said: 'Souls are prone to greed; from it is what she chose, and from it is what she did not choose.'"

In *Tafsir al-'Ayyashi*, from Hisham ibn Salim from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah: **وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ** — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager..." (4:129), he said: "In affection (المودة)."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Nuh ibn Shu'ayb and Muhammad ibn al-Hasan, who said: "Ibn Abi al-'Awja’ asked Hisham ibn al-Hakam, saying to him: 'Is Allah not Wise?' He said: 'Yes, He is the Wisest of judges.' He said: 'Then inform me about His saying: **فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً** — "...then marry of women as may be agreeable to you, two, or three, or four; but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one." Is this not an obligation?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Then inform me about His saying: **وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ** — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager. So do not incline with a total inclination, leaving her like one suspended." What kind of Wise One speaks like this?' Hisham had no answer.

So he traveled to Madinah to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: 'At a time other than Hajj or 'Umrah?' He said: 'Yes, may I be your ransom, for a matter that concerned me. Ibn Abi al-'Awja’ asked me a question for which I had nothing.' He said: 'And what is it?' So he informed him of the story.

Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said to him: 'As for the saying of Him, the Mighty and Majestic: **فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً** — "...then marry of women as may be agreeable to you, two, or three, or four; but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one," it means in maintenance (النفقة). And as for His saying: **وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ** — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager. So do not incline with a total inclination, leaving her like one suspended," it means in affection (المودة).'

He said: When Hisham returned to him with this answer and informed him, he said: 'By Allah, this is not from you!'"

I say: A similar hadith was also narrated from al-Qummi that some heretics (الزنادقة) asked Abu Ja'far al-Ahwal about the exact same issue. He traveled to Madinah and asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about it, and he answered him with the same answer. Abu Ja'far returned to the man and informed him, and he said: "This you have carried from the Hijaz."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ** — "...leaving her like one suspended" (4:129)—meaning you leave the one you do not incline toward like one who is neither having a husband nor unmarried. He said: "It is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him)." In the same source, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): that he used to divide [his time] among his wives and say: **اللهم هذه قسمتي فيما أملك فلا تلمني فيما تملك ولا أملك** — "O Allah, this is my division in what I possess, so do not blame me for what You possess and I do not possess."

I say: The non-Shi'i scholars (الجمهور) narrated it through several chains. What is meant by his saying "what You possess and I do not possess" is heart-felt love. However, the narration is not free from some doubt, for Allah is too Majestic to blame anyone for what they do not possess at all, and He, the Exalted, has said: **لا يُكَلِّفُ اللهُ نَفْساً إِلَّا ما آتاها** — "Allah does not task any soul except [according to] what He has given it" (65:7). The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is more knowledgeable of the station of his Lord than to ask Him to bring about what already exists.

In *al-Kafi*, narrated from Ibn Abi Layla, who said: "'Asim ibn Humayd narrated to me, saying: 'I was with Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) when a man came to him and complained of need. He commanded him to marry. He said: Then his need intensified. He came to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) who asked him about his state. He said: "My need has intensified." He said: "Separate." So he separated. He said: Then he came to him and he asked him about his state. He said: "I have become wealthy and my state has improved." Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "I commanded you with two things that Allah commanded. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **وَأَنْكِحُوا الْأَيامى مِنْكُمْ وَالصَّالِحِينَ مِنْ عِبادِكُمْ وَإِمائِكُمْ** — 'Marry off those of you who are single, and those of your male and female slaves who are righteous...' until His saying: **وَاللهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ** — '...and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing' (24:32). And He said: **وَإِنْ يَتَفَرَّقا يُغْنِ اللهُ كُلًّا مِنْ سَعَتِهِ** — 'But if they separate, Allah will provide for each out of His bounty' (4:130).'"'
